level_description.overview: The Year 7 English curriculum focuses on developing students' knowledge, understanding and skills in listening, reading, viewing, speaking, writing and creating. Students interact with others in familiar and unfamiliar contexts, including local community contexts.
level_description.key_ideas: Students engage with a variety of texts for enjoyment and to inform. They develop an understanding of how language features, text structures and images shape meaning and influence audiences.
level_description.note: The curriculum is organized into three interrelated strands: Language, Literature and Literacy.

Language variation and change.description: Understand that Standard Australian English is one of many varieties of English used around the world

Text structure and organisation.description: Understand that the coherence of complex texts relies on devices that signal sequences and build connections

Literature and context.description: Identify and explore ideas and viewpoints in a variety of literary texts drawn from different historical, social and cultural contexts

Interpreting, analysing, evaluating.description: Analyse the ways language is used to express and clarify expectations and values in texts

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 7, students understand how text structures and language features help to achieve particular effects. They explain how the choices made by authors influence personal responses. They create texts for different purposes and audiences.

level_description.overview: In Year 8, students listen to, read, view, interpret, evaluate and create a range of spoken, written and multimodal texts. These include various types of media and literary texts that challenge and explore themes.
level_description.key_ideas: Focus on how language is used to present different points of view and how creators of texts use language features to influence the audience's response.
level_description.note: Learning in English is recursive and cumulative.

Language for interaction.description: Understand the use of punctuation and how it assists in the creation of meaning

Responding to literature.description: Explore and explain the combinations of language and visual choices that authors make to present information

Creating texts.description: Create imaginative, informative and persuasive texts that raise issues and report on events

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 8, students explain how ideas are represented and how texts reflect or challenge contexts. They explain the aesthetic qualities of texts and how text structures shape meaning.

level_description.overview: Year 9 students experience learning in familiar and unfamiliar contexts, including local or global community and vocational contexts. They create increasingly complex texts.
level_description.key_ideas: Analyzing the ways in which language and images are used to create character, setting and plot in different forms of literature.
level_description.note: Strands: Language, Literature, Literacy.

Expressing and developing ideas.description: Analyse how higher order concepts are developed in complex texts through language features

Interpreting, analysing, evaluating.description: Evaluate the use of evidence and the logic of arguments in a range of persuasive texts

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 9, students analyze how ideas are portrayed and how texts are influenced by contexts. They explain how authors create nuance and complexity in their work.

level_description.overview: In Year 10, students evaluate how text structures and language features can be used to influence audiences and represent viewpoints. They engage with a wide range of sophisticated texts.
level_description.key_ideas: Critically analyzing the influence of context, purpose and audience on the development of texts.
level_description.note: Integration of the three strands.

Literature and context.description: Evaluate the social, moral and ethical positions represented in texts from different cultures and times

Interacting with others.description: Identify and explore the purposes and effects of different types of language used in public debate

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 10, students evaluate how text structures and language features influence interpretations. They demonstrate a high level of control over language choices to create impact.

level_description.overview: The Year 7 curriculum focuses on number and place value, algebra, and the introduction of variables. Students develop their understanding of fractions, decimals, and percentages.
level_description.key_ideas: Proficiency strands: Understanding, Fluency, Problem-solving and Reasoning.
level_description.note: Content strands: Number and Algebra, Measurement and Geometry, Statistics and Probability.

Number and place value.description: Investigate and represent whole numbers as products of powers of prime numbers
Investigate everyday situations that use integers. Locate and represent these numbers on a number line

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 7, students solve problems involving the comparison, addition and subtraction of integers. They make connections between whole numbers and index notation and represent numbers as products of prime factors.

level_description.overview: The proficiency strands understanding, fluency, problem-solving and reasoning are an integral part of mathematics content across the three content strands: number and algebra, measurement and geometry, and statistics and probability. The proficiencies reinforce the significance of working mathematically within the content and describe how the content is explored or developed.
level_description.key_ideas: At this year level: understanding includes describing patterns in uses of indices with whole numbers, recognising equivalences between fractions, decimals, percentages and ratios, plotting non-linear relationships, and identifying conditions for congruence of triangles.
level_description.note: The mathematics content at this year level involves three strands: Number and Algebra, Measurement and Geometry, and Statistics and Probability.

Number and place value.description: Use index notation with numbers to establish the index laws with positive integral indices and the zero index

Real numbers.description: Carry out the four operations with rational numbers and integers, using efficient mental and written strategies and appropriate digital technologies

Money and financial mathematics.description: Solve problems involving profit and loss, with and without digital technologies

Linear and non-linear relationships.description: Plot linear relationships on the Cartesian plane with and without the use of digital technologies
Solve linear equations using algebraic and graphical techniques. Verify solutions by substitution

Using units of measurement.description: Choose appropriate units of measurement for area and volume and convert from one unit to another
Find perimeters and areas of parallelograms, trapeziums, rhombuses and kites

Geometric reasoning.description: Define congruence of plane shapes using transformations

Chance.description: Identify complementary events and use the sum of probabilities to solve problems

Data representation and interpretation.description: Investigate techniques for collecting data, including census, sampling and observation
Explore the practicalities and implications of obtaining data through sampling using a variety of investigative processes

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 8, students solve everyday problems involving rates, ratios and percentages. They recognise index laws and apply them to whole numbers. They describe rational and irrational numbers. Students solve problems involving profit and loss. They make connections between expanding and factorising algebraic expressions. Students solve linear equations and graph linear relationships on the Cartesian plane. They convert between units of measurement for area and for volume, and perform calculations to determine perimeter and area of parallelograms, rhombuses and kites. They identify conditions for congruence of triangles and deduce the properties of quadrilaterals. Students model authentic situations with two-way tables and Venn diagrams. They choose appropriate language to describe events and experiments.

level_description.overview: Year 9 focuses on expanding knowledge of indices, scientific notation, and non-linear relationships. Students are introduced to basic trigonometry.
level_description.key_ideas: Applying index laws, sketching parabolas, and calculating surface area and volume of composite solids.
level_description.note: Proficiencies are integrated into all strands.

Pythagoras and trigonometry.description: Investigate Pythagoras' Theorem and its application to solving simple problems involving right-angled triangles

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 9, students solve problems involving simple interest and apply index laws to numerical expressions. They find the surface area and volume of right prisms and cylinders.

level_description.overview: Year 10 involves higher-level algebraic expansion and factorisation, quadratics, and advanced probability. Students study bivariate data and circular functions.
level_description.key_ideas: Solving quadratic equations, determining probabilities of independent and dependent events, and calculating surface area/volume of spheres and pyramids.
level_description.note: Prepares students for senior secondary mathematics.

Patterns and algebra.description: Factorise algebraic expressions by taking out a common algebraic factor

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 10, students recognize the connection between simple and compound interest. They expand binomial expressions and solve simple quadratic equations. They determine the surface area and volume of pyramids and spheres.

level_description.overview: In Year 7, students explore the diversity of life on Earth and the role of classification. They investigate the properties of mixtures and how they can be separated.
level_description.key_ideas: Systems involve interactions between components; change can be caused by forces like gravity.
level_description.note: Science inquiry skills and human endeavour are addressed across Years 7-8.

Biological sciences.description: There are differences within and between groups of organisms; classification helps organise this diversity

Chemical sciences.description: Mixtures, including solutions, contain a combination of pure substances and can be separated using a range of techniques

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 7, students describe techniques to separate mixtures and identify physical and chemical changes. They predict the effect of environmental changes on feeding relationships.

level_description.overview: In Year 8, students are introduced to cells as microscopic structures that explain macroscopic properties of living systems. They link form and function at a cellular level and explore the organisation of body systems in terms of flows of matter between interdependent organs. Similarly, they explore changes in matter at a particle level, and distinguish between chemical and physical change. They begin to classify different forms of energy, and describe the role of energy in causing change in systems, including the role of heat and kinetic energy in the rock cycle.
level_description.key_ideas: Students use experimentation to isolate relationships between components in systems and explain these relationships through increasingly complex representations. They make predictions and propose explanations, drawing on evidence to support their views while considering other points of view.
level_description.note: The science inquiry skills and science as a human endeavour strands are described across a two-year band and should be addressed across Years 7-8.

Biological sciences.description: Cells are the basic units of living things; they have specialised structures and functions
Multi-cellular organisms contain systems of organs carrying out specialised functions that enable them to survive and reproduce

Chemical sciences.description: Properties of the different states of matter can be explained in terms of the motion and arrangement of particles
Differences between elements, compounds and mixtures can be described at a particle level
Chemical change involves substances reacting to form new substances

Earth and space sciences.description: Sedimentary, igneous and metamorphic rocks contain minerals and are formed by processes that occur within Earth over a variety of timescales

Physical sciences.description: Energy appears in different forms, including movement (kinetic energy), heat and potential energy, and energy transformations and transfers cause change within systems

Nature and development of science.description: Scientific knowledge has changed peoples' understanding of the world and is refined as new evidence becomes available
Science knowledge can develop through collaboration across the disciplines of science and the contributions of people from a range of cultures

Use and influence of science.description: Solutions to contemporary issues that are found using science and technology may impact on other areas of society and may involve ethical considerations
People use science understanding and skills in their occupations and these have influenced the development of practices in areas of human activity

Questioning and predicting.description: Identify questions and problems that can be investigated scientifically and make predictions based on scientific knowledge

Planning and conducting.description: Collaboratively and individually plan and conduct a range of investigation types, including fieldwork and experiments, ensuring safety and ethical guidelines are followed
Measure and control variables, select equipment appropriate to the task and collect data with accuracy

Processing and analysing data and inform.description: Construct and use a range of representations, including graphs, keys and models to represent and analyse patterns or relationships in data using digital technologies as appropriate
Summarise data from students' own investigations and secondary sources, and use scientific understanding to identify relationships and draw conclusions based on evidence

Evaluating.description: Reflect on scientific investigations including evaluating the quality of the data collected and identifying improvements
Use scientific knowledge and findings from investigations to evaluate claims based on evidence

Communicating.description: Communicate ideas, findings and evidence based solutions to problems using scientific language and representations, using digital technologies as appropriate

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 8, students explain how differences in cells may relate to their function, and describe the organisation of body systems. They describe patterns of change and compare physical and chemical change. They explain energy transformations and transfers and describe the role of energy in forming geological features. They describe factors that can influence the development of science understanding, and explain how science is used to inform decisions and actions.

level_description.overview: Year 9 students explore ecosystems, atomic theory, and chemical reactions. They study the theory of plate tectonics and how it explains geological activity.
level_description.key_ideas: Matter and energy flow through systems. Understanding the structure of the atom is key to chemical behavior.
level_description.note: Science inquiry skills and human endeavour are addressed across Years 9-10.

Physical sciences.description: Energy transfer through different mediums can be explained using wave and particle models

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 9, students explain chemical processes and natural radioactivity in terms of atoms and the subatomic particles they contain. They explain how the theory of plate tectonics explains global patterns of geological activity.

level_description.overview: Year 10 focuses on genetics, evolution, the periodic table, and the Big Bang theory. Students apply laws of physics to motion and forces.
level_description.key_ideas: The universe is a vast system governed by physical laws. Evolution explains the diversity of life over time.
level_description.note: Integration of global systems and human impacts.

Earth and space sciences.description: The Big Bang theory can be used to explain the origin of the universe

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 10, students analyze how the periodic table is used to predict the properties of elements and their chemical reactions. They explain the origin of the universe and how evolution has occurred through natural selection.

level_description.overview: A study of history from the time of the earliest human communities to the end of the ancient period, c.60,000 BC – c.650 AD.
level_description.key_ideas: Ancient world overview including Egypt, Mesopotamia, Greece, Rome, and China.
level_description.note: Focus on historical knowledge and skills.

Overview of the ancient world.description: The theory that people moved out of Africa between 120,000 and 60,000 years ago

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 7, students describe the effects of change on societies and individuals. They identify the motives and actions of people in the ancient world.

level_description.overview: The Year 8 curriculum provides a study of history from the end of the ancient period to the beginning of the modern period, c.650–1750 AD (CE). This was when major civilisations around the world came into contact with each other. Social, economic, religious and political beliefs were often challenged and significantly changed. It was the period when the modern world began to take shape.
level_description.key_ideas: The content provides opportunities to develop historical understanding through key concepts, including evidence, continuity and change, cause and effect, perspectives, empathy, significance and contestability.
level_description.note: The history content at this year level involves two strands: historical knowledge and understanding, and historical skills. These strands are interrelated and have been developed to be taught in an integrated way, and in ways that are appropriate to specific local contexts.

Overview of the ancient to modern world.description: The transformation of the Roman world and the spread of Christianity and Islam
Key features of the medieval world (feudalism, trade routes, voyages of discovery, contact and conflict)
The emergence of ideas about the world and the place of people in it by the end of the period (such as the Renaissance, the Scientific Revolution and the Enlightenment)

The Western and Islamic world.description: The way of life in Medieval Europe (social, cultural, economic and political features) and the roles and relationships of different groups in society
Significant developments and/or cultural achievements, such as changing relations between Islam and the West (including the Crusades), architecture, medieval manuscripts and music

The Asia-Pacific world.description: The way of life in shogunate Japan, including social, cultural, economic and political features

Chronology, terms and concepts.description: Sequence historical events, developments and periods

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 8, students recognise and explain patterns of change and continuity over time. They explain the causes and effects of events and developments. They identify the motives and actions of people at the time. Students explain the significance of individuals and groups and how they were influenced by the beliefs and values of their society. They describe different interpretations of the past.

level_description.overview: A study of the history of the making of the modern world from 1750 to 1918.
level_description.key_ideas: Industrial Revolution, movement of peoples, and World War I.
level_description.note: Focus on patterns of change and continuity.

The Industrial Revolution.description: The nature and significance of the Industrial Revolution and how it affected living conditions

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 9, students refer to key events and actions to explain patterns of change. They analyze the causes and effects of industrialization and global conflict.

level_description.overview: A study of the history of the modern world and Australia from 1918 to the present.
level_description.key_ideas: World War II, the Cold War, and the struggle for rights and freedoms.
level_description.note: Emphasizes Australia's global context.

Global conflict.description: The causes and course of World War II, including the experience of Australians

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 10, students evaluate the significance of individuals and groups in shaping the modern world. They analyze different interpretations of the past.

level_description.overview: Two units: 'Water in the world' and 'Place and liveability'.
level_description.key_ideas: Water as a renewable resource and the factors that influence the liveability of places.
level_description.note: Interrelated strands of knowledge and skills.

Water in the world.description: The classification of environmental resources and the forms that water takes

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 7, students describe geographical processes that influence the characteristics of places. They explain the interconnections between people and environments.

level_description.overview: There are two units of study in the Year 8 curriculum for Geography: 'Landforms and landscapes' and 'Changing nations'. 'Landforms and landscapes' focuses on investigating geomorphology through a study of landscapes and their landforms. 'Changing nations' investigates the changing human geography of countries, as revealed by shifts in population distribution.
level_description.key_ideas: A framework for developing students' geographical knowledge, understanding and skills is provided through the inclusion of inquiry questions and specific inquiry skills, including the use and interpretation of maps, photographs and other representations of geographical data.
level_description.note: The content of this year level is organised into two strands: geographical knowledge and understanding, and geographical inquiry and skills. These strands are interrelated and have been developed to be taught in an integrated manner, and in ways that are appropriate to specific local contexts.

Unit 1: Landforms and landscapes.description: Different types of landscapes and their distinctive landform features
Geomorphic processes that produce landforms, including a case study of at least one landform

Unit 2: Changing nations.description: Causes and consequences of urbanisation, drawing on a study from Indonesia, or another country of the Asia region
Reasons for, and effects of, internal migration in both Australia and China

Observing, questioning and planning.description: Develop geographically significant questions and plan an inquiry using appropriate geographical methodologies and concepts

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 8, students explain geographical processes that influence the characteristics of places and explain how places are perceived and valued differently. They explain interconnections within environments and between people and places and explain how they change places and environments. They compare alternative strategies to a geographical challenge, taking into account environmental, economic and social factors.

level_description.overview: Two units: 'Biomes and food security' and 'Geographies of interconnections'.
level_description.key_ideas: Distribution of biomes and the challenges of feeding a growing global population.
level_description.note: Focus on sustainability and interconnectedness.

Biomes and food security.description: The distribution and characteristics of biomes as regions with distinctive climates and vegetation

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 9, students explain how geographical processes change the characteristics of places. They analyze strategies to improve food security.

level_description.overview: Two units: 'Environmental change and management' and 'Geographies of human wellbeing'.
level_description.key_ideas: Management of environmental change and the variations in human wellbeing between places.
level_description.note: Focus on global issues and management strategies.

Environmental change.description: The human-induced environmental changes and their impacts on ecosystem services

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 10, students evaluate environmental management strategies and explain variations in human wellbeing across different regions.

level_description.overview: Focus on the key features of Australia's system of government and the Constitution.
level_description.key_ideas: Protections provided by the Constitution and the principle of justice.
level_description.note: Knowledge and skills are taught in an integrated way.

Government and democracy.description: The key features of the Australian Government's system, including the Constitution

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 7, students explain features of Australia's Constitution and how the legal system is based on the principle of justice.

level_description.overview: The Year 8 curriculum provides a study of the responsibilities and freedoms of citizens and how Australians can actively participate in their democracy. Students consider how laws are made and the types of laws used in Australia. Students also examine what it means to be Australian by identifying the reasons for and influences that shape national identity.
level_description.key_ideas: A framework for developing students' civics and citizenship knowledge, understanding and skills at this year level is provided by the following key questions: What are the freedoms and responsibilities of citizens in Australia's democracy? How are laws made and applied in Australia? What different perspectives are there about national identity?
level_description.note: The civics and citizenship content at this year level involves two strands: civics and citizenship knowledge and understanding, and civics and citizenship skills. These strands are interrelated and have been developed to be taught in an integrated way.

Government and democracy.description: The freedoms that enable active participation in Australia's democracy within the bounds of law, including freedom of speech, association, assembly, religion and movement
How citizens can participate in Australia's democracy, including use of the electoral system, contact with their elected representatives, use of lobby groups, and direct action

Laws and citizens.description: How laws are made in Australia through parliaments (statutory law) and through the courts (common law)
The types of law in Australia, including criminal law and civil law, and the place of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander customary law

Citizenship, diversity and identity.description: Different perspectives about Australia's national identity, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives, and what it means to be Australian

Questioning and research.description: Develop a range of questions to investigate Australia's political and legal systems

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 8, students analyse features of Australian democracy, and explain features of Australia's democracy that enable active participation. They recognise different types of law in Australia and explain how laws are made. They identify the diverse belief systems in Australia and analyse issues about national identity and the factors that contribute to people's sense of belonging.

level_description.overview: Understanding Australia's political system and how it enables change through parties and interest groups.
level_description.key_ideas: Influence of the media and the key principles of the justice system.
level_description.note: Focus on active participation.

Laws and citizens.description: The key principles of Australia's justice system, including the right to a fair trial

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 9, students evaluate features of Australia's political system and analyze the influences on people's political choices.

level_description.overview: Comparing systems of government and analyzing Australia's global roles and responsibilities.
level_description.key_ideas: The role of the High Court and international obligations.
level_description.note: Advanced study of democratic values.

Government and democracy.description: The key features and values of Australia's system of government compared to at least one other system

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 10, students compare systems of government and analyze the Australian Government's global roles.

level_description.overview: Exploring how resources are allocated and the interdependence of consumers and producers.
level_description.key_ideas: The role of businesses and the characteristics of entrepreneurs.
level_description.note: Knowledge and skills strands.

Resource allocation.description: The way resources are allocated in an economy to meet needs and wants

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 7, students describe the interdependence of consumers and producers in the market and the reasons businesses exist.

level_description.overview: The Year 8 curriculum gives students the opportunity to further develop their understanding of economics and business concepts by exploring the ways markets – including traditional Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander markets – work within Australia, the participants in the market system and the ways they may influence the market's operation.
level_description.key_ideas: The rights, responsibilities and opportunities that arise for businesses, consumers and governments are considered along with the influences on the ways individuals work now and into the future.
level_description.note: The economics and business content at this year level involves two strands: economics and business knowledge and understanding, and economics and business skills. These strands are interrelated and have been developed to be taught in an integrated way, and in ways that are appropriate to specific local contexts.

Resource allocation and making choices.description: The ways markets in Australia operate to enable the distribution of resources, and why they may be influenced by government
The traditional markets of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities and their participation in contemporary markets

Consumer and financial literacy.description: The rights and responsibilities of consumers and businesses in Australia in terms of financial and economic decision-making

Business environment.description: Types of businesses and the ways that businesses respond to opportunities in Australia

Work and work futures.description: Influences on the ways people work and factors that might affect work in the future

Questioning and research.description: Develop questions about an economic or business issue or event, and plan and conduct an investigation or project

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 8, students explain how markets operate and recognise why governments may influence the market's operation. They explain the rights and responsibilities of consumers and businesses in terms of financial and economic decision-making. They explain why different types of businesses exist and describe the different ways businesses can respond to opportunities in the market. Students describe influences on the way people work and factors that may affect work in the future.

level_description.overview: Focus on the performance of the economy and the factors that influence living standards.
level_description.key_ideas: Managing financial risks and rewards and the role of the government in the economy.
level_description.note: Integration of financial literacy.

Economic performance.description: Indicators of economic performance and how they examine the state of the economy

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 9, students explain how the performance of the economy is measured and analyze the role of participants in the global economy.

level_description.overview: Exploring the global economy and the ways in which businesses manage their workforces.
level_description.key_ideas: Impact of global trends and the future of work.
level_description.note: Focus on management and global markets.

Global economy.description: The impact of the global economy on Australia's economic performance

achievement_standard.summary: By the end of Year 10, students analyze how the global economy affects Australia and explain the factors that influence business decision-making.
